title: Preparing for a hosepipe ban
subtitle: Water storage, soil mulching, and prioritising which plants need water when supply is restricted
excerpt: Hosepipe bans are enforced by water companies under the Water Industry Act 1991 when reservoir levels drop below trigger points. In south-east England they are increasingly common in summer. Preparation involves maximising water storage capacity before a ban, mulching to cut evaporation, and knowing which plants can survive two to four weeks without supplemental water.
sourceNotes: Water Industry Act 1991 Section 76 for hosepipe ban powers. RHS drought-tolerant gardening guidance. Anglian Water, Thames Water, and Southern Water hosepipe restriction FAQs. Garden Organic no-dig mulching guides. WRAP water-smart gardening guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Collecting rain from a roof surface via gutters and downpipes into a water butt or tank for garden or non-potable household use.

content.text: A hosepipe ban under the Water Industry Act 1991 bans using a hosepipe connected to the mains for garden watering, car washing, or pool filling. Water cans and buckets filled at the tap remain permitted during a ban: the restriction is on the hosepipe connection, not on manual watering. Preparation before a ban is announced is more effective than response after it starts.
Increasing water storage before the ban
Water butts: a 210-litre butt fills from a standard roof in a moderate shower (12 mm on a 30 m2 roof section yields 360 litres). Fit extra butts on unused downpipes in spring.
is best, but used bath water and rinse water can be bucketed to established ornamental plants during a ban. Use within a day to avoid bacterial build-up.
Washing machine water: final rinse water from a washing machine can be diverted to the garden using a length of pipe and a bucket. Suitable for established shrubs and trees; avoid on food crops.

A 50-100 mm mulch layer (compost, wood chip, leaf mould, or straw) on all bare soil reduces evaporation by 50-70% compared to bare ground in direct sun. Apply in late spring before the soil dries out: mulching dry soil locks in drought, not moisture. Water the bed first, then mulch. Mulching around the base of container plants (but not touching the stem) reduces watering frequency from daily to every 2-3 days in typical summer conditions.
Water these first: containers (they dry out fastest), seedlings under 6 weeks old, new perennials and shrubs in their first summer, and fruiting crops at flowering stage.

Water these last, or skip them: lawns go brown but recover in autumn. Mature shrubs and trees find moisture with their deep roots. Drought-tolerant borders such as lavender, verbena, and sedums need little water.
Let these go: annual bedding plants that are past their peak (replace after the ban lifts), cool-season crops that have bolted (lettuces, spinach in mid-summer).
